meta.title: Roman Catholic View of Justification (Part 2): Luther and the Reformation
meta.author: R. C. Sproul / Ligonier Ministries

questions.question: The Council of Trent met in which Italian city?
Teaching associated with the Council of Trent is often described by which adjective?
Which session of the Council of Trent is said to be most relevant to the doctrine of justification in this lecture?
The sixth session of Trent is divided into which two main parts (as described)?
options: Debates on Marian dogma; then papal supremacy
Formal teaching on justification; then canons/anathemas
Only historical preface; no doctrinal content
A decree on Scripture alone; then a decree on tradition alone
questions.answer: Formal teaching on justification; then canons/anathemas

questions.question: The recurring formula for many Trent canons is best summarized as:
options: If anyone denies tradition, let him be blessed
If anyone reads the Greek New Testament, let him be silent
If anyone says X, let him be anathema
If anyone preaches without a degree, let him be suspended
questions.answer: If anyone says X, let him be anathema

questions.question: The first three canons of Trent’s sixth session are said to be chiefly directed against:
According to the speaker, some Trent canons aimed at reformers missed because:
options: The reformers were not yet born
There were misunderstandings implied in the anathemas
Trent only addressed the Eastern Orthodox
questions.answer: There were misunderstandings implied in the anathemas

questions.question: The speaker argues that if the Reformation doctrine of justification is correct, then to anathematize it is to:
On justification, the speaker claims there can be no rapprochement between Rome and Protestants unless:
options: one side surrenders, because the positions are incompatible
questions.answer: one side surrenders, because the positions are incompatible

questions.question: Which New Testament letter does the speaker cite for “another gospel” and anathema—even if an angel preaches it?
Rome’s sixth session is summarized as teaching that faith is a _____ condition for justification.
According to the lecture’s summary of Rome, faith is described as NOT being which kind of condition for justification?
The lecture uses oxygen and fire primarily to illustrate:
options: the difference between necessary and sufficient conditions
questions.answer: the difference between necessary and sufficient conditions

questions.question: In Trent’s treatment of losing justification through mortal sin (as summarized), a person may:
options: retain real faith yet lose justification
lose both faith and baptismal water
questions.answer: retain real faith yet lose justification

questions.question: The causal framework used in the lecture to explain Rome’s “instrumental cause” language is associated with:
In the statue illustration, the stone or marble out of which the statue is made is called the:
In the statue illustration, the sculptor who actually brings about the shaping is the:
In the statue illustration, the chisel and hammer are grouped under:
According to the Roman Catholic view summarized here, the instrumental cause of justification is:
The Reformation claim previewed at the end contrasts Rome by saying the sole instrumental cause is:
options: faith in the heart of the believer
questions.answer: faith in the heart of the believer

questions.question: The speaker labels “2 + 2 = 4” as an example of:
The speaker uses “a bachelor is an unmarried man” to show that in an analytical statement:
options: the predicate adds nothing new beyond analyzing the subject
marriage is a sacrament that removes sin
questions.answer: the predicate adds nothing new beyond analyzing the subject

questions.question: In the Roman scheme summarized as “analytical justification,” God is said not to declare a person just until:
options: the person completes 40 days of fasting only
under analysis the person is actually just / righteousness inheres
questions.answer: under analysis the person is actually just / righteousness inheres

questions.question: In the summary given, dying with any imperfection on the soul leads to needing _____ before heaven, in Rome’s framework.
